meta.model: claude-sonnet-5 (manual, no API key — see CLAUDE.md v4 workaround)

digests.narrative:
His highest-conviction, highest-concentration theme. Core thesis: NVIDIA's move to 1.6T optics and co-packaged optics (CPO) is forcing an architectural shift from EML to continuous-wave (CW) lasers, creating a severe laser chokepoint. He believes Sivers ($SIVE) is the under-recognized CW/DFB laser bottleneck feeding the whole hyperscaler CPO ecosystem — Ayar in NVDA's NVLink scale-up, POET for scale-out, Jabil for 1.6T pluggables, O-Net for ELS — at ~60% gross margins with large TAM optionality. He holds a million-plus share position.

Around it he tracks AAOI (CW-laser LTAs with AMD/CSPs, citing ~800%+ Y/Y potential), LITE and COHR (top-three laser suppliers 'completely sold out' into 2028-2029), JBL (1.6T LRO built around Sivers lasers) and POET (CPO scale-out, top-3 hyperscaler customer via Lumilens). His timing map: 1.6T pluggables now->H1 2027, CPO scale-up late 2026, CPO scale-up H2 2027 — Taiwan CPO names are early (and he's down on them) but he treats the recent selloff as noise vs. a ~$154B optical / ~$91B CPO TAM by 2028.
keyPoints: CW laser chokepoint: NVDA's 1.6T/CPO shift forces EML->CW lasers; he sees Sivers ($SIVE) as the hidden bottleneck feeding the whole hyperscaler ecosystem.
Sivers maps to Ayar (NVLink CPO scale-up), POET (scale-out), Jabil (1.6T pluggables) and O-Net (ELS) — ~60% gross margins with large TAM optionality personal million-plus share position.
AAOI: CW-laser LTAs with AMD/CSPs, cites ~800%+ Y/Y growth potential and rising $471M/month projections into 2028.
LITE/COHR: top-three laser suppliers 'completely sold out' into 2028-2029; Jabil's 1.6T LRO built around Sivers lasers.
Timing map: 1.6T pluggables now->H1 2027, CPO scale-out late H2 2026, NPO H2 2026/2027, CPO scale-up H2 2027; Taiwan CPO names are early (and down) but expected to recover.
Highest-concentration theme — treats the recent photonics selloff as short-term memory loss vs. a ~$154B optical / ~$91B CPO TAM by 2028.

digests.narrative:
Lighter conviction than photonics, more research-note in tone. AXTI signed a 3-year wafer supply deal with Coherent — a $22.29M prepayment for committed capacity — but its rapid re-rating ($13 -> $140s) came with a float expansion and dilution he flags as a reason for some caution on retail-facing upside.

He sees this month's capital rotation moving from photonics into memory (MU, SNDK) as the market's main focus, expecting it to rotate back eventually since photonics remains his highest-conviction theme. A newer research thread traces a tungsten (WF6) export-control bottleneck out of China that could pressure Japan/Korea DRAM and NAND supply chains via suppliers like Kanto Denka and Peric Special Gases.
keyPoints: AXTI signed a 3-year wafer supply deal with Coherent — a $22.29M prepayment for committed capacity.
AXTI's rapid re-rating ($13 -> $140s) came with float expansion and dilution, warranting some caution.
Capital rotated into memory (MU, SNDK) this month while photonics corrected — he expects rotation back into photonics eventually.
New bottleneck thread: Chinese tungsten (WF6) export controls could pressure Japan/Korea DRAM and NAND supply.

digests.narrative:
TSM is read as aggressively building a silicon-photonics/CPO empire through its subsidiaries Xintec (testing) and VisEra (optical foundry), with the chairman confirming CoPoS pilot lines running now for volume in 2-3 years — a longer runway than the market's 'heavily delayed' narrative suggests. Reports also name TSM as a materials-analysis client for Intel, ASML, Applied Materials and others via a small supplier (MSSCORPS).

Separately, he flags that OpenAI's reported talks to give the US government a stake echo the Intel playbook — a potential soft government backstop for hyperscaler capex obligations. The recurring tungsten (WF6) export-control thread also touches fab-adjacent DRAM/NAND supply chains in Japan and Korea.
keyPoints: TSM is aggressively building a silicon-photonics/CPO empire via subsidiaries Xintec (testing) and VisEra (optical foundry); CoPoS pilot lines are running now for 2-3 year volume.
OpenAI's reported talks to give the US government a stake echo the Intel playbook — a potential soft government backstop for hyperscaler capex.
TSM named as a materials-analysis client for Intel, ASML, and Applied Materials via a small under-the-radar supplier.
The tungsten (WF6) export-control bottleneck out of China is a recurring watch item for Japan/Korea fab-adjacent supply chains.

digests.narrative:
High conviction that the 'Meta is cutting capex' narrative is disinformation. His read: Google restricted Meta's internal compute allocation back in March because Meta's own usage was too high, forcing Meta to immediately sign $48B+ contracts with neoclouds like CoreWeave and Nebius — a demand-constraint story, not a demand-weakening one. Meta then announced plans to build its own compute-resale cloud business (stock up 6.65% premarket), which he reads as neoclouds like Nebius being the ones actually powering that offering.

He also treats a Bloomberg report that crashed semis 7-18% (UBS: 'this is not new news') as manufactured noise rather than a thesis break, and cites Wells Fargo's read that Meta's excess-compute plans validate rather than threaten the neocloud opportunity.
keyPoints: Google restricting Meta's internal compute allocation in March forced Meta's $48B+ neocloud contracts (CRWV, NBIS) — a demand-constraint story, not a 'cutting capex' story.
Meta's plan to resell excess compute (stock +6.65% premarket) is read as neoclouds like Nebius powering that offering.
Wells Fargo: Meta's excess-compute plans validate the AI-infra opportunity for neoclouds rather than threatening it.
A Bloomberg report crashed semis 7-18% on framing UBS called 'not new news' — treated as a buying window, not a thesis break.

digests.narrative:
Lighter-touch theme, mostly picks-and-shovels research rather than headline conviction. AEHR's ~100% rally is viewed as largely priced in, with limited near-term re-rating room absent a new catalyst. CPO remains flagged as his '#1 favorite theme' for H2 2026, with materials/substrate names positioned as classic picks-and-shovels exposure that sells into every chip regardless of who wins the CPO race.

Ongoing deep-dive research (trade-record digging into Starlink and other direct supplier chains) continues to surface obscure, under-the-radar materials names like LPK. The tungsten (WF6) export-control thread out of China is a recurring materials-bottleneck watch item feeding into DRAM/NAND supply chains.
keyPoints: AEHR's ~100% rally is seen as mostly priced in — limited near-term re-rating room without a new catalyst.
CPO is flagged as his '#1 favorite theme' for H2 2026; materials/substrate names are classic picks-and-shovels exposure.
Ongoing trade-record research (e.g. Starlink supply chains) surfaces obscure materials names like LPK.
The China->Japan->Korea tungsten (WF6) export-control thread remains a recurring materials-bottleneck watch item.

digests.narrative:
Centers on a half-serious 'conspiracy theory' that Nvidia is discretely backing ASIC challengers — Marvell, MediaTek, AlChip, GUC (NVLink) — to chip away at Broadcom's custom-silicon share, the same way neocloud offerings created a moat against CSPs' custom ASICs last year. He notes the overall ASIC market grows regardless, but reads the challenger cohort as taking outsized share.

Jensen Huang's 'the next $1T company' comment continues to anchor sentiment through Marvell drawdowns. Marvell also sits inside the broader 1.6T/CPO supplier cluster (GFS, Jabil, Sivers) that underpins his core photonics thesis, and was part of the basket that sold off -11.2% on the recent Bloomberg-driven semis crash.
keyPoints: Half-serious thesis: Nvidia is discretely backing ASIC challengers (Marvell, MediaTek, AlChip, GUC) to chip away at Broadcom's custom-silicon share.
Jensen Huang's 'the next $1T company' comment continues to anchor sentiment through MRVL drawdowns.
Marvell sits inside the same 1.6T/CPO supplier cluster (GFS, Jabil, Sivers) underpinning his core photonics thesis.

digests.narrative:
High conviction, and his largest position by his own account: Agility Robotics (ticker changing $CCXI -> $AGLT on its ~September IPO) is 'literally the #1 humanoid player in the US in terms of commercialization today,' ranked ahead of Tesla Optimus and Figure ($39B last round). BlueCrest Capital / Michael Platt disclosed a 5.6% stake in CCXI — institutional validation he'd been expecting.

His bull case: ~75% US-sourced parts, a <$30K bill-of-materials target (vs. a ~$145K sticker price people over-focus on), and 10,000-unit RoboFab capacity across Salem, Pittsburgh and Fremont, backed by Amazon, SoftBank, Foxconn and Nvidia. He frames the field via an LLM-era analogy — Agility is 'Anthropic for robotics,' Optimus is xAI, Figure is ChatGPT/Microsoft — and cites China's 54%+ share of global robotics deployments as the reason a 'national robotics strategy' narrative is building. He prefers CCXI's valuation discipline (~$4.9-5.5B) over richer peers like $BOT, which trades well above NAV.
keyPoints: Agility Robotics ($CCXI -> $AGLT on IPO) is his largest position — 'the #1 US humanoid player in commercialization today,' ranked ahead of Tesla Optimus and Figure.
BlueCrest Capital / Michael Platt disclosed a 5.6% stake in CCXI, an institutional validation signal.
Bull case: ~75% US-sourced parts, targeting a <$30K BOM (vs. ~$145K sticker), and 10,000-unit RoboFab capacity across three US sites.
Frames the field via an LLM-era analogy: Agility is 'Anthropic for robotics,' Optimus is xAI, Figure is ChatGPT/Microsoft.
China's 54%+ share of global robotics deployments is driving a 'national robotics strategy' narrative in the US.
Prefers CCXI's valuation discipline (~$4.9-5.5B) over richer-valued peers like $BOT, which trades well above NAV.

digests.narrative:
High conviction that the CW-laser chokepoint is an Nvidia-and-AMD-shared problem: both are hunting for long-term laser agreements as the top-three suppliers run sold out into 2028-2029. AAOI's 800%+ Y/Y growth case is explicitly tied to AMD's CW LTA activity and ~$471M/month demand scaling into 2028, with ASPs and margins expected to rise industry-wide as CSPs compete for remaining supply.

He treats a SemiAnalysis report framing CPO as delayed — paired with a rival optics ETF launch right after a 40-60% optical drawdown — as manufactured FUD that Nvidia and analyst desks refuted. He also reads Goldman's NVDA/AVGO ecosystem note as a map for finding 'hidden gem' small-cap suppliers (PCL, MSSCorp, Nextronics) institutions haven't priced in yet, and frames the broader AI compute race in explicitly geopolitical terms — a 'modern cold war' — as the reason a hyperscaler capex pullback is hard to imagine.
keyPoints: AMD and Nvidia are both hunting for CW-laser long-term agreements as the top-three laser suppliers run sold out into 2028-2029.
AAOI's 800%+ Y/Y growth case is tied directly to AMD's CW LTA activity and ~$471M/month demand scaling into 2028.
A SemiAnalysis report framing CPO as delayed (paired with a rival ETF launch after a 40-60% optical drawdown) is read as manufactured FUD that Nvidia and analyst desks refuted.
Goldman's NVDA/AVGO ecosystem note is used as a map of 'hidden gem' small-cap suppliers institutions haven't priced in yet.
Frames the AI compute buildout as a 'modern cold war,' making a hyperscaler capex pullback hard to imagine.

digests.narrative:
High conviction that hyperscaler capex holds, built on the same read as his neoclouds thesis: Google restricted Meta's internal compute allocation in March, forcing Meta's $48B+ neocloud contracts — a compute-constraint story, not a 'Meta cutting capex' story the market misread. Amazon is explicitly his favorite hyperscaler: ~1.57M headcount as an LLM-automation opportunity feeding into physical AI (self-driving delivery, warehouse robotics) on top of AWS's own Trainium compute buildout.

He also flags Meta's Vistara architecture (older DDR4 memory with CXL expansion) as active cost engineering under compute constraints, and leans on geopolitical framing — China's claimed 'AI cyber nuclear weapon' capability — to argue a broad hyperscaler capex pullback is very unlikely while the AI race is read as a modern cold war.
keyPoints: Google restricting Meta's internal compute allocation in March forced Meta's $48B+ neocloud contracts — a compute-constraint story, not a 'cutting capex' story.
Amazon is his favorite hyperscaler: ~1.57M headcount as an LLM-automation opportunity, feeding into physical AI (delivery, warehouse robotics) plus AWS Trainium buildout.
Meta's Vistara architecture (older DDR4 + CXL memory expansion) is read as active cost engineering under compute constraints.
Geopolitical AI-race framing (China's capability claims) is used to argue a hyperscaler capex pullback is unlikely.
